entries.name: The Great Square
entries.firstApp: Chapter 8 (named; the Presentation is staged here ~30 days on)

fields.label: First appearance
fields.value: Chapter 8 (named; the Presentation is staged here ~30 days on)

sections.body: The principal public concourse at the [[the-concordance|Concordance]]'s seat in the [[heartland]] — the largest gathering-place the order controls, and therefore where the heartland is shown things it must all witness at once. Coined and approved as canon 2026-06-19 (Stephen). Its load-bearing role in Book 1 is as the venue of the **Presentation** (see [[state-of-the-world]]): the fixed-date public ceremony at which [[evan|Evan]] must be displayed and confirmed as Bearer before the whole heartland — the book's ticking clock made physical.

A vast open square; the single space in the order's seat that can hold a continent's worth of witnesses. Established detail (Ch. 8): [[evan|Evan]] has filed the **crowd-orders** for lesser days held here and knows "to the head" what the Square holds — his clerk's-eye measure of the thing, dread rendered as arithmetic. (Distinct from [[the-cradle]], the sound-swallowing *sacred* hall where prophecies are received and Bearers are *named*; the Great Square is the secular, public stage where a Bearer is *shown*.)

- **The Presentation (Ch. 14–15 per the beat sheet).** Staged here to display the obedient, prophesied Bearer; the establishment expects a sign/working, and the crowd expects to be reassured. It is the stage on which Evan, per locked canon, will instead use his irrevocable authority to slip the leash (declare for the [[the-tangle|Tangle]], not the south). See [[state-of-the-world]], `Notes/outline.md` Part II.
- Concordance-controlled public space; access, staging, and crowd-handling are the order's to manage (and the records of past gatherings pass through clerks like Evan).

- **Exact location.** Sits at the Concordance's heartland seat; whether that seat is a named capital city (and how far it lies from [[the-cradle]]) is **not yet established** — do not invent. The naming rite was held at the Cradle; the Presentation is here.
- Physical particulars (size, surrounding buildings, the dais/stage used for the Presentation) — fill as the manuscript fixes them.

- Coined in prose, `Manuscript/ch08.md` (2026-06-19); approved as canon by Stephen, 2026-06-19.
- Role builds on [[state-of-the-world]] (the Presentation as the public ceremony / ticking clock) and the locked two-ceremony structure ([[augurite]]).

Physical particulars (size, surrounding buildings, the dais/stage used for the Presentation) — fill as the manuscript fixes them.

entries.name: The North

sections.body: The cold northern reach of [[verranth]]: boreal forest and taiga, long winters, thin and hardy population. Peripheral to the heartland's prophecy order; defined for completeness and later use.
Dark conifer forest, frozen rivers, short fierce summers. Logging, furs, iron from the hills; isolated holds rather than cities.
[TBD — frontier, refuge, or source of a threat. Held as a region to develop when the plot needs it.]
- Peoples, polities, and what (if anything) the story needs from the North.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
- codex-world-builder Layer 1.

threads: Peoples, polities, and what (if anything) the story needs from the North.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
codex-world-builder Layer 1.

entries.name: The South

sections.body: The warm southern reach of [[verranth]]: a subtropical/mediterranean coast, a southwestern coastal desert, and the volcanic **Cinderfoot** at the southern end of the [[hagback-mountains]]. Held as a region to develop later.

Sun-baked coast and olive-and-vine country grading into desert on the western shore; inland, the black sand, sulfur, obsidian, and tremors of the Cinderfoot, where an ocean plate dives under the land.

[TBD — trade-rich coast, distinct southern culture/power. Developed when needed.] Strategically, the Cinderfoot is the continent's main source of [[firesalt]] (see below), which alone gives the South leverage over the firesalt-poor [[heartland]].
The Cinderfoot is the primary source of [[firesalt]] — the mineral the [[dray-drakes]] burn to power the magitech — plus obsidian, sulfur, alum, and geothermal heat. See [[resources-trade-goods]].
- Southern peoples and polities (Layer 3/4); the role of Cinderfoot obsidian and volcanism (Layer 2/9).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
- codex-world-builder Layer 1.

threads: Southern peoples and polities (Layer 3/4); the role of Cinderfoot obsidian and volcanism (Layer 2/9).
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
codex-world-builder Layer 1.

entries.name: The Sundering Sea

sections.body: The western ocean off [[verranth]] — the sea the prevailing westerlies cross before striking the [[hagback-mountains]], and the continent's main maritime trade space. An offshore archipelago and the strait beside it guard the approaches to the [[river-cessa]] delta, so whoever holds them taxes seaborne trade into the heartland.

Broad, weather-driven western waters; a scatter of islands (working: "the Sherds") across a navigable strait that funnels shipping toward the delta port. Fishing fleets, merchantmen, and drake-rigged coasters.

- Sea route into the heartland and a natural chokepoint at the strait.
- Likely home to a maritime power or free-port culture distinct from the inland heartland.
- Storm seasons roughen the crossing but, per the mild-climate decision, don't hard-stop it (see [[geography-climate-verranth]]).

- Who holds the archipelago/strait, and whether it's a kingdom, a league of free ports, or pirates (Layer 4).
- Names of the islands and the delta port.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
- codex-world-builder Layer 1; harbour/strait logic per worldbuilding-fundamentals.

threads: Who holds the archipelago/strait, and whether it's a kingdom, a league of free ports, or pirates (Layer 4).
Names of the islands and the delta port.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
codex-world-builder Layer 1; harbour/strait logic per worldbuilding-fundamentals.

entries.name: The Tangle

sections.body: A vast temperate forest, hard to clear and sparsely settled — an avoidance zone with old things in it. In a high-magic world, the Tangle is where the wild, dangerous kin of domesticated creatures (see [[dray-drakes]]) and other uncanny things persist.
Deep, close-canopied old-growth; little daylight at the floor; few roads and fewer that are safe. The kind of place the heartland's tidy prophecy order does not reach.

**A key destination (set 2026-06-18).** The Tangle is where the native way of magic survives — the home of the [[briarkin|Briarkin]], who never took the firesalt shortcut. At the climax, [[aldous-pemberquick|Pemberquick]] points [[evan|Evan]] here; Evan connects the dots by accident and kindles his first fuel-less light. So the Tangle is the seat of the cure to the [[the-long-dark|Long Dark]] — the antithesis of the firesalt heartland: un-industrialized, un-bureaucratized, where magic is still done the way it is meant to be. The road bends here (LOCKED 2026-06-18): everyone champions the South, but Evan slips the leash at the Presentation and steers the party to the Tangle; the South is the red herring.
- What lives in the Tangle; whether it has any inhabitants/keepers; its link to the magic system.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
- codex-world-builder Layer 1.

threads: What lives in the Tangle; whether it has any inhabitants/keepers; its link to the magic system.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (continent layout approved).
codex-world-builder Layer 1.

entries.name: Verranth

sections.body: The continent the story takes place on (working name). Roughly Europe-sized, temperate-to-subtropical, and — crucially — a place whose institutions, faiths, and politics are organized around prophecy. The [[heartland]] in the wet west is its civilizational center and the seat of the prophecy bureaucracy; the drier, colder, and stranger regions ring outward from there.

A central mountain spine ([[hagback-mountains]]) divides a green, river-fed west from a dry eastern steppe. North into cold taiga ([[the-north]]); south into subtropical coast and a volcanic district ([[the-south]]); a salt basin in the deep interior ([[the-glass-sea]]); a magic-thick forest ([[the-tangle]]); and the [[the-sundering-sea]] to the west with an offshore archipelago guarding the trade approaches. Magitech texture everywhere: drake-lamps on the post roads, message-drakes overhead (see [[dray-drakes]]).
The stage for Book 1. The "wrong man" premise plays out against a continent that genuinely runs on prophecy — so a broken prophecy is not a private problem but an infrastructural one.

- High-magic world; magic does much of industry's work (magitech patchwork). Full system in Layer 9.
- Climate is mild as a plot constraint (see [[geography-climate-verranth]]).

- Final continent name and the names of its regions (all working).
- The political map (Layer 4) and which powers hold which regions.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (seed: continental, comedic high-magic, magitech patchwork; continent layout approved).
- codex-world-builder Layer 1.

threads: Final continent name and the names of its regions (all working).
The political map (Layer 4) and which powers hold which regions.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (seed: continental, comedic high-magic, magitech patchwork; continent layout approved).
codex-world-builder Layer 1.

entries.name: The Cinderfoot Cities

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: independent of the [[the-concordance]]; holds the firesalt the heartland needs

sections.body: The independent firesalt ports and mining-cities of the volcanic south, run by [[southerners]] and [[emberkin]] together. Rich, irreverent, and very aware that the devout heartland cannot keep its lamps lit or its sacred post flying without southern [[firesalt]].
A loose league of self-governing cities (merchant councils/magistracies), cooperating on trade and defense, competing on everything else. Power is commercial: control of firesalt supply and price.
The South's leverage over the heartland is a standing pressure (and a tempting weapon if relations sour). A region that does not care about prophecy and finds the whole crisis mostly an opportunity.

- How unified the cities actually are, and who leads the league (if anyone).
- The split of firesalt wealth/power between southern humans and Emberkin.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (South = free firesalt-cities).
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

threads: How unified the cities actually are, and who leads the league (if anyone).
The split of firesalt wealth/power between southern humans and Emberkin.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (South = free firesalt-cities).
codex-world-builder Layer 4.

entries.name: The Eastland Confederation

fields.label: Leadership
fields.value: none standing; clan chiefs, uniting under a war-leader only in crisis

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: independent of the [[the-concordance]] and its faith; controls eastern firesalt/salt roads

sections.body: The loose political body of the steppe: the [[eastland-clans]] bound by custom, kinship, and mutual interest rather than a throne. Fractious in peace, formidable when a strong figure unites them. Sits astride the eastern routes the heartland's [[firesalt]] travels.
Decentralized; chiefs and councils; power is personal and shifting. A standing leader is the exception, usually forged by an external threat or a charismatic unifier.
Outside pressure on the heartland-centered story: a skeptical neighbor with leverage over firesalt, a source of allies/foils for Evan, and a wildcard if someone unites it.

- Is anyone currently uniting the clans? (Potential Layer 11 tension.)
- Their stance if the heartland's prophecy order visibly falters.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (East = clan confederation); Book 1 heartland-centered.
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

threads: Is anyone currently uniting the clans? (Potential Layer 11 tension.)
Their stance if the heartland's prophecy order visibly falters.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (East = clan confederation); Book 1 heartland-centered.
codex-world-builder Layer 4.

entries.name: The Free Companies

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: for hire; loyal to the contract and the paymaster

sections.body: The professional mercenary companies that do most of the heartland's fighting. Not one body but many competing outfits, each with its own captain, reputation, and price. When the Concordance or a house needs an army, it hires one.
Independent companies of varying size and specialty (infantry, drake-borne supply trains, siege crews). Power is reputation and the contract; they can be bought, outbid, or — if unpaid — turned.

The heartland's hired muscle: a source of contract intrigue, divided loyalties, and the unsettling fact that whoever pays best commands the swords. (Goals & trajectory: get paid, survive, build reputation; avoid the losing side.)

- Named companies and captains (Layer 12).
- Whether someone in the Evan crisis hires a company to settle it quietly — and whether the Vigil's oath collides with hired steel.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: heartland armies are mostly mercenaries.
- codex-world-builder Layer 7.

threads: Named companies and captains (Layer 12).
Whether someone in the Evan crisis hires a company to settle it quietly — and whether the Vigil's oath collides with hired steel.

entries.name: The Free-Port League

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: independent; shares the strait with the [[meren]]; profits from heartland trade and smuggling

sections.body: The political body of the archipelago: a league of independent free ports run by the human [[archipelago-folk]], coexisting (and bargaining) with the amphibious [[meren]] over the strait that all seaborne trade into the heartland must cross. Loyal to profit and home port before any crown or creed.

Loose league of self-governing ports; collective muscle when the strait or their trade is threatened, rivalrous otherwise. Power is control of the sea route — and of the smuggling that beats the heartland's tariffs.
Gatekeepers of seaborne firesalt and trade; a venue for intrigue, smuggling, and neutral ground. Outside pressure, not a central player in Book 1.

- The terms of their condominium with the Meren over the strait.
- Whether the League is a real polity or a convenient fiction over squabbling ports.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (Archipelago = free-port league).
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

threads: The terms of their condominium with the Meren over the strait.
Whether the League is a real polity or a convenient fiction over squabbling ports.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (Archipelago = free-port league).

entries.name: House Tarr

fields.label: Leadership
fields.value: the head of House Tarr (Cassian's parent/elder — TBD)

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: aristocracy of the [[the-concordance]]; rivals among the other great houses; aggrieved at [[evan]]

sections.body: A great martial house of the heartland and one of the Concordance's pillar aristocracies. For a generation House Tarr bet everything — money, marriages, training, reputation — on the certainty that the great prophecy would fall on their son [[cassian-tarr]]. It didn't. It fell on a clerk. The house's humiliation is total, public, and dangerous.

- An old dynasty with lands, retainers, and martial prestige (the house breeds and trains warriors and champions).
- Power built on honor, arms, and proximity to the [[the-concordance]] — all of it now wobbling on the failed prophecy bet.

- **Succession:** by blood within the house (rite TBD); Cassian was raised as the heir to its destiny, not merely its lands.
- **Reach into the Concordance:** House Tarr is one of the great houses that steer the Council of Readers from behind (patronage, marriage, placed clients). This is exactly why losing the Naming stings doubly — the house that helped run the machine was overruled by the machine, and by [[law-of-prophecy]] cannot simply reverse it.
- Honor, lineage, destiny earned through training and blood. They believed prophecy rewards the *worthy* — and they made Cassian worthy. The reassignment is, to them, an offense against merit itself.

As the foremost martial house, Tarr keeps real, well-trained house troops and retinues — unusual in a heartland that mostly hires its wars from the [[free-companies]]. This martial strength is a genuine lever: when the Concordance needs steel it must call on houses like Tarr, and a humiliated, armed house is a dangerous thing (see [[warfare-of-verranth]]).

A wounded, motivated power: the house wants the prophecy (and its standing) back, by reclaiming, discrediting Evan, or proving Cassian's right. (Goals & trajectory: restore honor and position; recover the destiny; survive the humiliation without losing rank among the houses.)

- [[cassian-tarr]] (the would-be Chosen One)
- The head of the house and kin — TBD (Layer 12).

- **Did House Tarr engineer the misassignment? ⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY: no — it was pure accident** (see [[prophecy-and-foresight]]). But the house is a prime *suspect* in everyone's eyes, and may well try to *fabricate* a culprit, or to alter/exploit the situation now. Their live secret is the **reclaim conspiracy** — how far they'll go to take the destiny back. Reveal path: discovered via house intrigue, a leaked plan, or Cassian's own conflict.
- House leadership and Cassian's exact standing within it now.
- Its rivalries with the other great houses.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: Cassian is a noble warrior-scion of a great martial house staked on the prophecy.
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

entries.name: The Merchant Houses

fields.label: Leadership
fields.value: the heads of the leading houses; no single ruler

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: profit-first; entangled with the [[the-concordance]] and the noble houses; rivals among themselves

sections.body: The great trading houses that run heartland commerce: warehouses, credit, long-haul contracts, and the firesalt import trade. Not noble in blood (though the lines blur by marriage and money), they are the day-to-day economic power of the [[heartland]] — quieter than the Concordance, often richer.

- A loose, competitive bloc of family firms; power measured in ships, warehouses, contracts, and outstanding loans.
- Control the import of [[firesalt]] and the credit that finances everyone else, including, at times, the Concordance and the noble houses.
- Profit and stability over piety. They want the trade routes open and the coin sound; the prophecy crisis interests them mainly as a risk to commerce — or an opportunity.

The money behind events; a source of leverage, bribes, information, and self-interested allies (or obstacles). (Goals & trajectory: keep trade flowing and the [[concord-mark]] sound; profit from volatility; avoid picking a losing side in the Evan crisis.)

- Their true relationship to the noble houses and the Concordance (rivals, partners, or overlapping families).
- Whether a house is quietly betting on — or against — Evan.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: merchant houses dominate commerce.
- codex-world-builder Layer 6.

threads: Their true relationship to the noble houses and the Concordance (rivals, partners, or overlapping families).
Whether a house is quietly betting on — or against — Evan.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: merchant houses dominate commerce.
codex-world-builder Layer 6.

entries.name: The Northern Holds

fields.label: Leadership
fields.value: hold-lords and clan elders; no central authority

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: independent and peripheral; indifferent-to-hostile toward the [[the-concordance]]'s faith

sections.body: The scattered political units of the taiga north: independent holds and clan-leagues of the [[northfolk]], with no central throne and little interest in the heartland's prophecy bureaucracy. Hard country, hard people, kept at arm's length from the continent's main quarrels.
Fragmented: each hold or league rules itself; alliances are seasonal and kin-based. Power is local — survival, furs, timber, iron, and the respect of neighbors.
Background for Book 1; a reserve of older beliefs and a possible refuge or wildcard later (especially given the nearby [[the-tangle]]).

- Whether the North matters to Book 1 at all, or is held for later.
- The northern beliefs and whether they're a truer counterpoint to the prophecy faith (Layer 8/13).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (North = independent holds).
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

threads: Whether the North matters to Book 1 at all, or is held for later.
The northern beliefs and whether they're a truer counterpoint to the prophecy faith (Layer 8/13).
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: outer powers as proposed (North = independent holds).
codex-world-builder Layer 4.

entries.name: The Concordance

fields.label: Leadership
fields.value: the Council of Readers (an oligarchy of senior interpreters); real power held behind it by the noble houses

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: governs the [[heartland]]; uneasy with the prophecy-sworn [[the-vigil]]; depends on (and looks down on) the firesalt powers of [[the-south]] and the east

sections.body: The prophecy bureaucracy — and, in the heartland, the government itself. The Concordance receives, records, interprets, and administers all recognized prophecy issued at [[the-cradle]]; because the continent treats prophecy as load-bearing infrastructure, the body that controls prophecy controls the heartland. It is exactly the institution Evan clerks for, which is what makes his selection so mortifying: the machine named one of its own filing-clerks.

- A vast civil service of clergy-clerks, archivists, interpreters, and inspectors layered over the old noble houses, who serve as its aristocracy.
- Controls [[the-cradle]] (the prophecy source) and the supply of [[augurite]].
- Real power lies in *interpretation and administration* — deciding what a prophecy means and who it names — far more than in armies. (Governance detail → Layer 5.)

- **The Council of Readers.** The Concordance is ruled not by one prophet but by an oligarchy of senior interpreters ("Readers") who authenticate prophecy and decide what it means. Decisions are collective, procedural, and slow — committee rule over fate itself.
- **Succession:** Council seats pass by election/co-option among the senior Readers (exact rite TBD), which makes the Council politically maneuverable.
- **Real power behind the throne:** the great noble houses (see [[house-tarr]]) shape the Council from behind — through patronage, marriage, and placing their people among the Readers. The clergy reads; the houses steer.
- **The law it administers:** the [[law-of-prophecy]] — receipt, Reading, Naming, and the irrevocable authority of the Bearer. It is precisely this law that now binds the Concordance to Evan.

- Prophecy is true, reliable, and to be obeyed *as administered*. The unspoken doctrine is that **the system cannot err** — which Evan's misassignment detonates.
- Internal splits (likely): true believers vs. cynical careerists; houses that want the "error" quietly fixed vs. those who insist the Cradle is infallible and Evan must therefore be the man.

Mints the [[concord-mark]] and taxes trade, pass-tolls, and the [[firesalt]] flow — its fiscal backbone. It does not run commerce directly; the [[merchant-houses]] do, and the Concordance leans on their credit. Its authority and its coin's value are quietly linked: a public failure of its core function could shake both (see [[economy-and-trade]]).

No large standing army of its own. The Concordance pays, contracts, and blesses — fielding force by hiring the [[free-companies]] and calling on the great houses' levies (foremost [[house-tarr]]). It prefers law, money, and spin to open war (see [[warfare-of-verranth]]). The [[the-vigil]] is independent of it and not its sword.

The central institution of Book 1: its crisis is the plot. Its instinct will be to manage, spin, or correct the embarrassment of Evan — while the prophecy-sworn [[the-vigil]] insists the named man must be protected. (Goals & trajectory: preserve its authority and the doctrine of infallibility; contain the Evan problem; keep firesalt flowing.)

- [[evan]] (a very junior clerk — until he isn't)
- [[doss]] (another junior clerk; Evan's friend)
- [[sabine-orrec]] (senior Reader of the Council; the face bearing down on Evan)
- Further high officials, interpreters, inspectors — TBD (Layer 12).

- **The Presentation (ticking clock):** it must publicly present Evan as Bearer on a fixed date or admit the unthinkable — and it is fighting the [[the-vigil]] for control of him meanwhile. See [[state-of-the-world]].
- Its leadership and exact governance (Layer 5).
- **Did someone inside the Concordance cause or cover the misassignment?** (Link [[the-cradle]], [[augurite]] — Layer 13.)
- How it handles the doctrine-shattering possibility that the Cradle was wrong.
- **The denied truth of the Long Dark.** Insiders roughly understand that the fires are failing from medium exhaustion by overuse — and the Concordance officially denies it, because the only real fix (*use less magic*) would gut the [[firesalt]] economy it taxes and undercut the faith that authorizes it. Its instinct is to treat the doom as the prophesied trial for the Bearer to heroically avert, not a systemic problem it must own. This willful blindness is a central Part III pressure. See [[the-long-dark]].

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: the Concordance IS the heartland state (theocratic prophecy-bureaucracy).
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

threads: **The Presentation (ticking clock):** it must publicly present Evan as Bearer on a fixed date or admit the unthinkable — and it is fighting the [[the-vigil]] for control of him meanwhile. See [[state-of-the-world]].
Its leadership and exact governance (Layer 5).
**Did someone inside the Concordance cause or cover the misassignment?** (Link [[the-cradle]], [[augurite]] — Layer 13.)
How it handles the doctrine-shattering possibility that the Cradle was wrong.

**The denied truth of the Long Dark.** Insiders roughly understand that the fires are failing from medium exhaustion by overuse — and the Concordance officially denies it, because the only real fix (*use less magic*) would gut the [[firesalt]] economy it taxes and undercut the faith that authorizes it. Its instinct is to treat the doom as the prophesied trial for the Bearer to heroically avert, not a systemic problem it must own. This willful blindness is a central Part III pressure. See [[the-long-dark]].

entries.name: The Vigil

fields.label: Allegiance / opposition
fields.value: sworn to **prophecy itself / its named bearers** — *not* to the [[the-concordance]], which is a source of friction

sections.body: The martial order that guards prophecy-bearers. Crucially, the Vigil is sworn to **prophecy itself**, not to the Concordance — its oath is to protect whoever the Cradle names. So when the prophecy lands on [[evan]], the Vigil is bound, by its deepest principle, to keep him alive — even as the Concordance might prefer the embarrassment quietly resolved. Tamsin Locke is the protector it assigns to him.

- A disciplined order of elite sworn protectors (and the trainers, quartermasters, and chapter-houses behind them).
- Authority comes from the oath and from tradition, not from the bureaucracy — which is exactly why it can defy the Concordance when the two disagree.

- **Command:** the order's own chapter command (titles TBD), independent of the Council of Readers.
- **Legal basis:** the oath rests on the [[law-of-prophecy]] — the Bearer must be protected. Because a Naming is irrevocable, the Vigil's duty to Evan is as binding as the law that named him, no matter how inconvenient the Concordance or the houses find it.

- The named bearer must be protected, full stop. The prophecy is sacred; the *bearer* is sacred; politics is not their concern.
- This makes them principled, stubborn, and a problem for anyone who finds the prophecy's choice inconvenient.

A small, elite order of sworn protectors — not a field army. The Vigil wins by skill, positioning, and the law behind it, not numbers; in open war it would be outmassed by the [[free-companies]] or a house's troops (see [[warfare-of-verranth]]). Its strength is that harming a Bearer means defying the law itself.

The institutional reason Evan is protected rather than disappeared, and a wedge between Evan and the Concordance. (Goals & trajectory: uphold the oath; protect the named bearer; preserve the order's independence from the Concordance.)

- [[tamsin-locke]] (assigned to Evan)
- [[edda-roun]] (Warden-Commander; gave Tamsin the assignment)
- Other peers and chapter command — TBD (Layer 12).

- **Live tension:** custody of Evan — the Vigil's oath to protect him collides with the [[the-concordance]]'s wish to manage/contain him ahead of the Presentation. See [[state-of-the-world]].
- Origin of the order and the exact wording/limits of its oath (does it bind even if the bearer is "wrong"? — pressure point).
- How far it will go against the Concordance to honor the oath.
- Its leadership.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: the Vigil is sworn to prophecy itself, not the bureaucracy; Tamsin is its assigned protector for Evan.
- codex-world-builder Layer 4.

threads: **Live tension:** custody of Evan — the Vigil's oath to protect him collides with the [[the-concordance]]'s wish to manage/contain him ahead of the Presentation. See [[state-of-the-world]].

How far it will go against the Concordance to honor the oath.

entries.name: Augurite

sections.body: The fate-material: a vanishingly rare, relic-grade substance tied to prophecy itself rather than to ordinary mining. Where [[firesalt]] is the mundane fuel of daily magitech, augurite is the sacred, dangerous stuff of fate — coveted, hoarded, and worth killing over. Seeded here as a plot object; its true properties are a deliberate mystery (Layer 9/13).

Established in prose (Ch. 2–3): a small rough nub that would fit in two cupped hands — "almost glass and almost ice and is neither." Light slides around it instead of landing. While charged with a fresh prophecy it carries a **"held-breath" quality** (the air of a room the instant before a door opens). It looks cold but is **warm on contact** — "warm as a hand" — and at the moment of naming the held quality lets go all at once: the Bearer's name arrives silently in every mind present (not spoken aloud), and the censers ring once. After it names, the warmth/charge drains — it has been "spent." (Forms only where fate is "thin," at or near [[the-cradle]].)

- **Working concept:** augurite can hold, carry, or reveal fragments of prophecy — a way to capture what is normally only "received" at the [[the-cradle]]. Exact powers and **hard limits** to be fixed with the magic system (Layer 9) so reveals play fair.
- It does not behave like a normal resource: it cannot simply be mined at scale, which is why it is relic-grade rather than a commodity.
- **Naming — GENERAL RULE (LOCKED, Stephen 2026-06-18).** A piece of augurite charged with a fresh prophecy at the [[the-cradle]] fixes that prophecy's **Bearer** on **contact** — it names **whoever touches it**. There is no discernment, no test of worth: first skin wins. Cast-facing, the faith reads this as fate guiding the right hand to the stone at the appointed moment. In Book 1's inciting event, [[evan|Evan]] trips and bumps the naming-stone holding [[the-long-dark]], and it names him.
- **The naming rite — LOCKED (Stephen 2026-06-18).** Because the rule is blind, a charged naming-stone is the most dangerous object in the heartland: anyone who brushes it becomes Bearer. The [[the-concordance|Concordance]] confers the role only through a **controlled naming rite** — a solemn but **restricted** ceremony before a few hundred (the Council, notables, and pilgrims admitted by lottery — ≈400 at [[cassian-tarr|Cassian]]'s rite, Ch. 2), **distinct from (and well before) the later mass public [[state-of-the-world|Presentation]] before thousands** — at which the single prepared, sanctioned candidate is brought forward to make contact. (Scale clarified 2026-06-19: the rite is exclusive, not small; the *Presentation* is the whole-heartland affair.) Tellingly, the rite polices *ceremonial contact* with great solemnity but handles the stone itself with casual institutional contempt: junior clerks and attendants do the menial tending and stand right beside it. That gap is the whole disaster.
- **How Evan was named (the inciting accident — LOCKED 2026-06-18).** The rite in question was [[cassian-tarr|Cassian]]'s. Evan, a junior clerk, was told to go stand by the augurite and mind it — the senior figures being far too important to bother with the stone themselves — and he trips and bumps it. It names him, an instant before it would have named Cassian. The scandal is total, and so is the irony: the hierarchy's own disdain for the clerk is precisely what hands him the prophecy. (The unanswerable tension stands — did the bump *cause* the naming, or did fate *arrange* the stumble? ⚠ author-only: blind accident.)
- **⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY:** that the stone names by blind contact is the buried proof there is no fixed Pattern — every Bearer in history, [[halden-the-vindicant|Halden]] included, was simply first to touch the rock. Do not let any character confirm this; see [[prophecy-and-foresight]], [[the-vindication]].
Forms in connection with [[the-cradle]]; historically gathered, regulated, or monopolized by the prophecy bureaucracy (TBD — Layer 5/8). Chain of custody to be built.

- **What augurite actually does**, and its hard limits (Layer 9) — do not resolve by invention.
- ~~Could augurite be involved in *how the prophecy landed on the wrong man*?~~ **RESOLVED (Stephen, 2026-06-18):** yes — augurite is the naming agent, and it named Evan by accidental contact (see Function / rules above). Not theft or forgery; a blind stone and a clumsy clerk.
- Who controls the known supply, and who wants it.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include a rare, relic-grade fate-material tied to the Cradle, seeded as an Object + Open Thread.
- codex-world-builder Layer 2.

entries.name: The concord-mark

sections.body: The heartland's standard coin: a metal piece struck and guaranteed by [[the-concordance]] and trusted as money across much of the continent. (Working name.)
Stamped with the Concordance's seal. [Denominations, metal, and design TBD.]

Ordinary money; its value rests on confidence in the Concordance. A blow to the Concordance's authority (say, a very public failure of its core function) could shake confidence in the coin too — a quiet economic stake in the prophecy crisis.
Minted by the Concordance; circulates continent-wide via the [[merchant-houses]] and trade routes (see [[economy-and-trade]]).
- Denominations and whether debasement/counterfeiting is a live issue.

entries.name: Archipelago folk

sections.body: The independent human sea-traders of the [[the-sundering-sea]] archipelago: cosmopolitan, free-port people who live by shipping, brokerage, and (when the tariffs bite) smuggling. They share the strait — sometimes as partners, sometimes as rivals — with the amphibious [[meren]], and together they sit on the seaborne route into the heartland.

- **Outlook:** worldly, flexible, loyal to the deal and the home port more than to any crown or creed.
- **Daily life:** crowded harbor towns, drake-rigged coasters, mixed crews; a place where every people and good on the continent passes through.
- **Relations:** indispensable middlemen to the heartland's seaborne trade (including smuggled [[firesalt]]); wary coexistence with the Meren over the strait.

- **Cultural voice (working):** sailing and trade idiom, many borrowed words, easy profanity, suspicion of zealots; a port-town cosmopolitan flatness of address.
- Their control of the strait and the smuggling trade is a direct lever on the firesalt dependency (Layer 6/11).

- Whether the archipelago is a league of free ports, a single maritime power, or pirate-ridden chaos (Layer 4).
- The exact terms of their condominium with the Meren over the strait.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: flesh the human regional peoples.
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

entries.name: Briarkin

sections.body: The fae-folk of the [[the-tangle]]: small, secretive, and the most naturally magic-adept people on Verranth — tricksters bound up with old magic and the wild drakes the heartland never tamed. To heartlanders they are half-legend, half-warning; you do not go into the Tangle, and you certainly do not bargain with what lives there.

- **Working traits:** diminutive, long-lived, glamour-wreathed; innate magic where heartlanders need study and firesalt; rarely seen outside the forest.
- **Relations:** they keep to the Tangle and its edges; outsiders who enter deal in bargains, riddles, and debts that tend to come due at the worst moment.
- **Magic angle (LOCKED 2026-06-18):** the Briarkin do magic **the way it is meant to be done** — a trained, innate relationship with [[the-quick|the Quick]], no fuel. They are not "extra-magical"; the heartland is *under*-magical, having replaced the discipline with a shortcut. Firesalt is that shortcut — a steroid (see [[the-craft]], [[firesalt]]). Because wild magic doesn't force the Quick with a fuel-burn, it never drove the atrophy now killing the heartland's fires.

- **Cultural voice (working):** indirection, riddles, the language of bargains and owed favors; nothing given freely, nothing said plainly.
- A reservoir of old, un-bureaucratized magic the prophecy establishment can neither file nor control — useful for Evan's story and for Layer 13.

- **Their bearing on the Long Dark — RESOLVED (2026-06-18):** the Briarkin are **immune to the doom** (their magic doesn't atrophy [[the-quick|the Quick]]) and are the living **proof + keepers of the cure** — that the firesalt shortcut was never necessary and magic can be done sustainably. This makes them a quiet threat to the firesalt economy and the faith, and a natural (reluctant, bargain-driven) source of the way back. The cure is not quick: the heartland would have to relearn a discipline it abandoned generations ago. See [[the-long-dark]], [[the-craft]].
- **How Book 1 uses them — RESOLVED (2026-06-18): they appear on the page.** [[aldous-pemberquick|Pemberquick]] points [[evan|Evan]] toward the Briarkin / the [[the-tangle|Tangle]] for the climax; Evan **connects the dots by accident** and kindles his first fuel-less light. Their reluctance and indirection (bargains, debts, riddles) are the obstacle — Evan likely gets less a lesson than a maddening half-answer he completes himself. **Open:** how much of the Tangle is shown, and the shape of whatever bargain/encounter delivers the spark.
- How magical, how numerous, how dangerous — and whether they are one people or many (set with Layer 9).
- Their stake in prophecy and the wild drakes (the wild kin presumably work magic the same native way — confirm).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include non-human peoples; Briarkin approved as the people of the Tangle.
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

entries.name: Cairn-folk

sections.body: One of Verranth's non-human peoples: a stony, long-lived highland folk of the [[hagback-mountains]] — master miners and masons, slow and literal-minded, who quarry the metals and help move the [[firesalt]] the rest of the continent runs on.

- **Working traits:** long-lived, patient, durable, gravely literal; unmatched at stone and ore; deeply unimpressed by the heartland's hurry and prophecy-talk.
- **Role:** the hands behind the mountains' wealth and a comedic register all their own (extreme literalism against the Narrator's grandeur and the heartland's piety).
- Define their actual nature (how non-human, how magical, lifespan, numbers) before promoting past seed — keep consistent with the magic system (Layer 9).

- Their precise nature (how non-human, how magical, lifespan, numbers) — set with Layer 9.
- Their politics with the pass-holders and the firesalt trade (Layer 4/6).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include a few non-human peoples (specifics proposed by the skill, pending confirmation).

entries.name: Calendar of Verranth

fields.label: Reveal level
fields.value: framework defined ~70%; month names partly TBD

sections.body: The heartland reckons time from the Founding of the [[the-concordance]]: dates are given in **Years of the Concordance (YC)**. The story opens in roughly **YC 489** (working). The Mythic Age before Year 1 is dated, vaguely, "Before Founding (BF)."

- **Year:** ~360 days, twelve months of ~30 days (working frame — keep simple and memorable).
- **Numbering:** counted from the Founding (Year 1 = [[issa-vell]] establishing the Concordance). "YC" forward; "BF" backward into myth.
- **Months/seasons (working):** named for the agricultural and liturgical cycle; key liturgical dates anchor the faith's festivals (the Reading, Tellingday — see [[the-pattern-faith]]). Specific month names TBD; name them when a scene needs a date.
- **Other reckonings:** the [[eastland-clans|East]] and [[northfolk|North]] keep their own counts (by chiefs/seasons), not YC — a quiet marker of who does and doesn't accept the Concordance's order.

- Seasons gate travel and harvest mildly (climate is "mild" as a plot force — see [[geography-climate-verranth]]); festivals fall on fixed dates the cast can attend or disrupt.
- Dating time itself from the Concordance is a soft instrument of its authority: to write a date is to acknowledge the order.
- Final month names and the exact opening date (YC 489 is a working figure).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: epoch = the Concordance's founding.
- codex-world-builder Layer 10 (calendar).

threads: Final month names and the exact opening date (YC 489 is a working figure).

entries.name: Dray-drakes

sections.body: The continent's signature domesticated creature and the visible face of its magitech: lesser drakes, bred dog-to-ox-sized, used as living infrastructure — haulage, lamplight, and message-running. Where Earth would have engines, post, and streetlamps, Verranth has drakes. Their wild kin in [[the-tangle]] and the Cinderfoot are another matter.

- **Breeds (working):** *dray-drakes* proper (ox-sized, pull barges and wagons); *lamp-drakes* (small, caged, give steady cold light on post roads and in cities); *post-drakes* (swift fliers carrying messages — the continent's "mail"). Refine in Layer 9 alongside the magic system.
- **Domestication:** long-bred and ubiquitous in the [[heartland]]; their care, guilds, and breeding are an ordinary part of life and economy (hooks for Layers 6/8).

- They are *lesser* drakes — not the dragons of legend. Hard limits (size ceiling, what their light/flame can and can't do, temperament) to be set with the magic system in Layer 9 so they don't become a too-convenient solution.
- Wild drakes (the Tangle, the Cinderfoot) are dangerous and untamed — the same lineage that makes life convenient in the heartland makes the wilds lethal.

- Whether drakes connect to the magic system proper or are "just animals" (Layer 9).
- The true nature of the wild/great drakes (possible Layer 13 material).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (approved the magitech-patchwork register and a signature fauna seed).
- codex-world-builder Layer 1.

threads: Whether drakes connect to the magic system proper or are "just animals" (Layer 9).
The true nature of the wild/great drakes (possible Layer 13 material).
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17 (approved the magitech-patchwork register and a signature fauna seed).
codex-world-builder Layer 1.

entries.name: Eastland clans

sections.body: The mobile horse peoples of the leeward steppe — clan-based, hardy, and pointedly skeptical of the heartland's prophecy religion. They are the continent's built-in outside perspective: a culture that does not assume fate is reliable, watching the heartland's certainties from across the mountains.

- **Outlook:** self-reliant, plain-spoken, suspicious of grand destiny; loyalty runs to clan and kin, not to a far-off prophecy.
- **Daily life:** herds, seasonal camps, riding; hard trading towns at the pass-mouths of the [[hagback-mountains]] and along the salt-and-firesalt roads to the [[the-glass-sea]].
- **Leverage:** they sit astride the overland routes the heartland's [[firesalt]] travels — useful friends, dangerous to cross.

- **Cultural voice (working):** metaphors of horses, weather, distance, and herds; blunt, dry humor; respect earned by deeds, not titles or prophecies. Refine for dialogue.
- A natural source of allies, foils, and skeptics for Evan — people unimpressed by a "Chosen One," which suits a man who never wanted to be one.
- **Belief (light):** they keep no [[the-pattern-faith|Pattern]] faith; they honor deeds, kin, and ancestors over any foretold place — which makes the steppe the friendliest ground on the continent for the [[the-unwritten]] heresy.

- Political organization: loose confederation, rival clans, or an emerging steppe power? (Layer 4.)
- Their actual theology — do they have a counter-belief to prophecy, or just disbelief?

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: humans + a few non-human peoples; moderate friction; the east is loosely outside the prophecy faith.
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

threads: Political organization: loose confederation, rival clans, or an emerging steppe power? (Layer 4.)
Their actual theology — do they have a counter-belief to prophecy, or just disbelief?

entries.name: Economy & trade of Verranth

sections.body: How money and goods actually move on Verranth. Built on the resource map (see [[resources-trade-goods]]): the heartland is rich in food and money but poor in [[firesalt]], so its economy is a constant exchange of grain, coin, and manufactured goods outward for firesalt and raw materials inward. Commerce is run day-to-day by powerful [[merchant-houses]]; the [[the-concordance]] mints the money and taxes the flow.

- **Currency:** the **[[concord-mark]]** — metal coin struck by the Concordance and stamped with its seal; the heartland's standard money, widely trusted across the continent.
- **Commerce:** dominated by [[merchant-houses]] (distinct from, and sometimes intertwined with, the noble houses) who own the warehouses, the credit, and the long-haul contracts.
- **Routes:** the [[river-cessa]] and its delta port for bulk river/sea trade; the [[hagback-mountains]] passes for overland firesalt and eastern goods; the [[the-sundering-sea]] strait for seaborne trade (and smuggling).
- **Banking/credit:** merchant-house credit and letters of exchange grease long-distance trade (depth TBD).
- **Taxation:** the Concordance taxes trade, pass-tolls, and the firesalt flow — a major source of its power.

- **Black market:** firesalt smuggling (to dodge tolls/tariffs) is the great illicit trade, running through the [[the-sundering-sea]] strait and the passes.
- **Vulnerability:** the whole economy rests on imported firesalt; a squeeze on supply or a coin crisis would bite hard (Layer 11 fuel).
- Resource-stressed conditions mean prices wobble and the merchant houses profit from the volatility.

- The merchant houses' relationship to the noble houses and the Concordance (rivals, partners, or the same families wearing two hats?).
- Banking depth; whether a debt/credit crisis is looming.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: Concordance-minted coin; merchant houses dominate commerce.
- codex-world-builder Layer 6.

threads: The merchant houses' relationship to the noble houses and the Concordance (rivals, partners, or the same families wearing two hats?).
Banking depth; whether a debt/credit crisis is looming.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: Concordance-minted coin; merchant houses dominate commerce.
codex-world-builder Layer 6.

entries.name: Emberkin

sections.body: The drake-blooded people of the volcanic South: scaled, fire-touched, and long-lived, with an inborn affinity for [[firesalt]] and the [[dray-drakes]] that run on it. They are, quietly, indispensable — the best firesalt-miners, refiners, and drake-handlers alive — which gives the firesalt-poor [[heartland]] every reason to need them and, being the heartland, every reason to look down on them.

- **Working traits:** scaled skin, heat-tolerance, faint inner fire; long-lived; temperamentally like banked coals — slow to flare, dangerous when they do.
- **Role:** concentrated in the Cinderfoot ([[the-south]]); their affinity makes them central to the firesalt trade and to safe drake-handling — economic leverage baked into their blood.
- **Relations:** depended upon and disdained by heartlanders ("scaled heathens"); proud of a worth the devout north won't admit.

- **Cultural voice (working):** metaphors of heat, fire, stone, and patience; measured speech with a banked-anger undertone; long memories for slights.
- Their indispensability + the heartland's contempt is a ready-made tension (Layer 11) and ties a whole people to the master firesalt dependency.

- The nature of their "drake blood" and how it links to the magic system / true drakes (Layer 9/13).
- Whether they could choke the firesalt supply if pushed — and whether anyone's tempting them to.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include non-human peoples; Emberkin approved (drake-blooded south).
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

threads: The nature of their "drake blood" and how it links to the magic system / true drakes (Layer 9/13).
Whether they could choke the firesalt supply if pushed — and whether anyone's tempting them to.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include non-human peoples; Emberkin approved (drake-blooded south).
codex-world-builder Layer 3.

entries.name: Firesalt

sections.body:
The mineral heartland magic runs on. Firesalt is the fuel of the everyday [[the-craft|Craft]] — practitioners burn it to work, and the [[dray-drakes|magitech]] (drake-light, drake-post, drake-haulage) is simply that Craft built into living engines. No firesalt, no *heartland* magic. It is the continent's fuel and its single most strategic commodity.

**It is also a shortcut (LOCKED 2026-06-18).** Firesalt is not the only way to do magic — it is a *steroid*. Innate "wild" magic (the [[briarkin]]) works [[the-quick|the Quick]] the trained, native way with no fuel at all; firesalt lets the quick-deaf force the same effects without the discipline, which is what let the heartland industrialize magic. The catch is the [[the-long-dark|Long Dark]]: chronic civilizational reliance on the shortcut has atrophied the Quick's natural responsiveness. So the crisis is *not* firesalt running out (supply is fine) — it's the shortcut finally failing. See [[the-craft]].

- **What it is:** a refined mineral (working concept) fed to drakes; they convert it into their light, flame, and the lift for flight. Crude firesalt is mined; refining it into safe, usable drake-feed is a trade in itself (guild hooks for Layer 6).
- **Where it comes from:** abundantly in the volcanic **Cinderfoot** ([[the-south]]); secondarily as evaporite beds at [[the-glass-sea]] in the eastern interior. The [[the-eastlands]] sit astride the overland routes from both.
- **Who needs it most:** the [[heartland]] — the densest, most magitech-dependent region — which has almost none of its own.

- **The dependency:** the prophecy-keeping heartland must import firesalt from the volcanic south and the half-heathen east. This is the master geopolitical lever of the setting — tariffs at the [[hagback-mountains]] passes, smuggling by sea through the [[the-sundering-sea]] strait, embargo as a weapon, and chronic resentment.
- **Scarcity:** demand outpaces easy supply (the continent is resource-stressed). Prices and access wobble; a disruption to firesalt is a disruption to daily life and to the sacred drake-post itself.
- **Hard limits (to set in Layer 9):** what firesalt-fed drakes can and cannot do, so the fuel can't trivially solve problems. Coordinate with the magic system.

- Resolved (Layer 9): firesalt is the fuel of human magic generally — see [[the-craft]]. (Innate "wild" magic, e.g. the [[briarkin]], needs none.)
- Could the heartland ever synthesize or find its own firesalt — and who would lose if it did?
- Relationship (if any) between firesalt and [[augurite]] / the [[the-cradle]]. (Default: unrelated; confirm.)

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: firesalt powers the drakes; heartland dependent on outside supply; resource-stressed.
- codex-world-builder Layer 2.

entries.name: Geography & climate of Verranth

fields.label: Reveal level
fields.value: defined ~70%; revealed on the page TBD

sections.body: The tectonic and climatic logic that ties Verranth's regions together. The continent sits in the temperate-to-subtropical band inside a westerly wind belt; a central mountain spine (the [[hagback-mountains]]) splits it into a wet, populous west and a dry, leeward east. This single fact drives where people live, what they grow, and who the prophecy-keeping heartland even talks to.

- **Plates:** A continental plate carries Verranth; an oceanic plate subducts beneath its southwestern edge, raising the southern end of the Hagback into the volcanic [[the-south|Cinderfoot district]] (earthquakes, ash, obsidian). The Hagback proper is a convergent-uplift range.
- **Winds & rain shadow:** Prevailing westerlies off the [[the-sundering-sea]] strike the Hagback's western slopes and drop their moisture — lush foothills and river valleys (the [[heartland]]). The leeward east lies in rain shadow: dry steppe and grassland (the [[the-eastlands]]).
- **Drainage:** Western slopes feed the [[river-cessa]] (to the Sundering Sea). The arid east drains inward via the River Aud to a terminal salt lake, [[the-glass-sea]] (an endorheic basin ringed by salt flats).
- **Latitude bands:** subarctic taiga in [[the-north]]; temperate heartland and forest in the center-west; subtropical/mediterranean and coastal desert in [[the-south]].

- **Climate is mild as a plot force (Stephen's direction):** seasons flavor travel and scenes but do **not** hard-gate the story. Winters dust the passes and storm seasons roughen the sea, but treat these as atmosphere, not walls — a deadline-driven prophecy is pressured by people and chance, not blizzards.
- Population clusters on the western rivers and coasts; the interior salt basin and the deep [[the-tangle]] are near-empty avoidance zones.
- The east's separation from the wet heartland is geographic, which is why the [[the-eastlands]] sit loosely outside the heartland's prophecy religion.

- Exact latitudes / continent dimensions (kept loose on purpose; tighten only if a scene needs it).
- Whether the magic system (Layer 9) bends any of this physics — e.g., the [[the-cradle]]'s "fate-thin" nature.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: continent shape approved; climate set to "mild."
- codex-world-builder Layer 1; worldbuilding-fundamentals (rain shadow, hydrology, latitude bands).

threads: Exact latitudes / continent dimensions (kept loose on purpose; tighten only if a scene needs it).
Whether the magic system (Layer 9) bends any of this physics — e.g., the [[the-cradle]]'s "fate-thin" nature.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: continent shape approved; climate set to "mild."

entries.name: Heartlanders

sections.body: The dominant human people of the fertile west and the carriers of the prophecy religion. Heartlanders treat fate as dependable infrastructure — prophecy is administered, filed, and trusted the way another world trusts the post or the power grid — and they quietly assume the continent's story is *theirs*. Both [[evan]] and [[cassian-tarr]] are Heartlanders, from opposite ends of its social ladder.

- **Outlook:** orderly, devout, prosperous, faintly smug; deep faith that the prophecy machinery works and that following it correctly is both duty and destiny.
- **Daily life:** river-valley farms and market towns; drake-lamps and drake-post (see [[dray-drakes]]); temples and prophecy-offices as ordinary civic fixtures.
- **Class range:** from great houses (House Tarr and its peers) down to the clerks and laborers who actually run the bureaucracy (Evan's world).

- **Cultural voice (working):** metaphors drawn from records, offices, and fate ("filed," "as written," "due"); polite, procedure-minded speech; verbal taboo around admitting the prophecy could be *wrong*. Refine when drafting dialogue.
- Their certainty is exactly what Evan's misassignment breaks — a whole people whose worldview assumes the system can't err, confronted with proof that it did.

- Internal divisions (devout vs. cynical, capital vs. provinces, great houses vs. bureaucracy) — develop with Layer 4/5.
- How heartlanders treat the peoples they depend on (the firesalt-holding south, the skeptical east).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: Evan and Cassian are Heartlanders; the heartland is the prophecy-cultured center.
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

threads: Internal divisions (devout vs. cynical, capital vs. provinces, great houses vs. bureaucracy) — develop with Layer 4/5.
How heartlanders treat the peoples they depend on (the firesalt-holding south, the skeptical east).

entries.name: History of Verranth (eras & the Founding)

fields.label: Reveal level
fields.value: official history defined ~60%; the true origin is the locked secret (author-only)

sections.body: How the present came to be — as the heartland tells it. The accepted history is the [[the-concordance|Concordance]]'s own, heavily shaped by it: much is myth, spin, and suppression. Time itself is counted from the Concordance's Founding (see [[calendar-of-verranth]]).

sections.h: How it works / what happened (the official eras)
sections.body:
- **The Mythic Age (Before Founding).** The [[the-cradle]] is said to have existed since time out of mind; scattered seers and oracles read it, and prophecy was local, contradictory, and often abused. Origins of the Cradle are lost in myth — no one can verify them. *(This is exactly where the truth hides.)*
- **The Founding (Year 1).** [[issa-vell]], the First Reader, unites the seers, centralizes all true reading of the Cradle, and establishes the Concordance and the [[law-of-prophecy]]. Prophecy becomes administered, singular, and lawful. Year 1 of the calendar.
- **The Vindication (early Concordance).** The defining event: a great prophecy spectacularly "came true," carried by the Bearer [[halden-the-vindicant]] — after which prophecy was unquestionable and the Concordance's authority absolute (see [[the-vindication]]).
- **The Long Concord (the magitech age).** Centuries of Concordance dominance; the [[firesalt]]-fueled [[the-craft|Craft]] industrializes into the everyday magitech; the Readers, the great houses, and the Vigil institutionalize. The order matures into the comfortable establishment of the present.
- **The Present (Year ~489).** The order at its complacent height — until a prophecy lands on the wrong man.

- Because the Concordance authored the record, grudges, alternative histories, and inconvenient facts are quietly missing — fertile ground for reveals.
- Each era explains a present fact: the Founding → why one body controls prophecy; the Vindication → why the faith is unquestionable; the Long Concord → why magitech and firesalt run everything.

- **⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY (locked):** the Cradle's true origin and nature are not what the official history claims — there is no fixed Pattern (see [[prophecy-and-foresight]]). The Mythic Age and the propaganda exist precisely to bury this.
- The real story of the Founding and the Vindication (vs. the official myth) — Layer 13.
- How other peoples (East, North, South) remember this history differently.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: ancient/mythic origins; calendar from the Concordance's founding; a great vindicating prophecy; heavily propagandized record.
- codex-world-builder Layer 10.

threads: **⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY (locked):** the Cradle's true origin and nature are not what the official history claims — there is no fixed Pattern (see [[prophecy-and-foresight]]). The Mythic Age and the propaganda exist precisely to bury this.
The real story of the Founding and the Vindication (vs. the official myth) — Layer 13.
How other peoples (East, North, South) remember this history differently.

codex-world-builder Layer 10.

entries.name: The law of prophecy

sections.body: The legal-religious framework by which the [[the-concordance]] turns a prophecy into law. Because the heartland treats prophecy as binding infrastructure, the rules for receiving, authenticating, and assigning a prophecy are the rules of the state itself. This is the machinery Evan's misassignment jams.

- **Receipt:** a prophecy is received at [[the-cradle]] and fixed into a charged piece of [[augurite]] (a naming-stone).
- **Reading:** the Concordance's interpreters — the **Readers** — authenticate and interpret it, and a **Council of Readers** governs the process. (This is also where the noble houses exert their real, behind-the-throne influence — see [[the-concordance]].)
- **Naming:** the Bearer is fixed by the charged stone itself — [[augurite]] names **whoever touches it** (no discernment), so naming is conferred through a controlled **rite** in which only a sanctioned candidate is permitted to make contact. The named Bearer is entered in the rolls, and from that moment the Naming has the force of sacred law. (Evan's misassignment is exactly this rite gone wrong — the wrong hand reached the stone; see [[the-long-dark]], [[augurite]].)
- **The Bearer's authority:** the named Bearer's word carries legal and sacred weight. In matters touching the prophecy the Bearer can, in principle, **outrank ordinary officials** — which is catastrophic when the Bearer is a junior clerk named [[evan]]. The Bearer must also be protected (the basis of [[the-vigil]]'s oath).

- **Irrevocable:** a Naming cannot be undone by any authority. **Only the Cradle can unname a Bearer** — and it has never done so.
- **Unprecedented:** misassignment has *never happened*. There is no procedure, no form, no precedent for "the Cradle named the wrong man." The Concordance is, by its own law, trapped with Evan.
- **Doctrine of infallibility:** admitting the Cradle erred is effectively heresy and institutional suicide — so the law gives the Concordance no clean way out, and the truth no safe way in.
- **The collision:** the houses hold the real power *through* the Council, but the law has just handed sweeping sacred authority to a man they cannot remove. Power and law now point in opposite directions.

- Can the Cradle be *made* to unname a Bearer — and what would that cost? (Layer 13.)
- The exact limits of the Bearer's authority (what Evan can and cannot legally compel).
- Whether the houses or the Council can engineer a loophole — or a fatal "accident" the Vigil's oath exists to prevent.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: Concordance ruled by a council of interpreters; naming irrevocable + unprecedented; bearer holds sweeping sacred authority; real power sits with the noble houses.
- codex-world-builder Layer 5.

threads: Can the Cradle be *made* to unname a Bearer — and what would that cost? (Layer 13.)
The exact limits of the Bearer's authority (what Evan can and cannot legally compel).
Whether the houses or the Council can engineer a loophole — or a fatal "accident" the Vigil's oath exists to prevent.

entries.name: The Meren

sections.body: The amphibious sea-people of the [[the-sundering-sea]] and its archipelago: aloof, patient, and mercantile, at home in water and on the tide-line both. Together with (and against) the human [[archipelago-folk]], they effectively control the strait that all seaborne trade into the heartland must pass.

- **Working traits:** amphibious, cool-blooded in temperament, long-lived; equally at ease underwater and at a trading table.
- **Role:** masters of the coastal and undersea routes; brokers, pilots, and gatekeepers of the strait — and, when it suits them, smugglers' partners.
- **Relations:** a wary, profitable coexistence with the human archipelago traders; indifferent to the heartland's prophecy religion except as a market.

- **Cultural voice (working):** tides, depth, currents, and patience; slow, deliberate speech; deals measured in seasons, not days.
- Their grip on the strait is a lever on the heartland's seaborne firesalt and trade — another pressure point for Layer 11.

- How alien they are (fully aquatic society below the waves?) and how much the reader sees.
- Their politics with the human archipelago-folk: alliance, rivalry, or uneasy condominium over the strait.

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include non-human peoples; the Meren approved (archipelago sea-people).
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

threads: How alien they are (fully aquatic society below the waves?) and how much the reader sees.
Their politics with the human archipelago-folk: alliance, rivalry, or uneasy condominium over the strait.
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: include non-human peoples; the Meren approved (archipelago sea-people).
codex-world-builder Layer 3.

entries.name: Northfolk

sections.body: The hard, isolated human people of the northern taiga: hold-and-clan folk who keep older beliefs than the heartland's prophecy faith and want little to do with southern certainties. Furs, timber, iron, and amber come south from them; very little of the heartland's piety goes north.

- **Outlook:** self-reliant, taciturn, superstitious in an older register — omens, the dead, and the dark of the woods, not filed prophecy.
- **Daily life:** scattered holds rather than cities; long winters; logging, trapping, mining, and wary trade at the forest and mountain edges (near [[the-tangle]]).
- **Relations:** peripheral and a little suspect to heartlanders; they regard the prophecy bureaucracy as flatlander nonsense.

- **Cultural voice (working):** weather, wood, ancestors, and endurance; short, blunt, understated; respect for what's survived, scorn for what's soft.
- An older, non-bureaucratic relationship to omen and fate — a counterpoint to the heartland's industrialized prophecy (useful for Layer 8/9).

- Political shape: independent holds, a northern kingdom, or clan leagues? (Layer 4.)
- What the Northfolk's older beliefs actually are, and whether they're *truer* than the heartland faith (Layer 8/13).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: flesh the human regional peoples.
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

threads: Political shape: independent holds, a northern kingdom, or clan leagues? (Layer 4.)
What the Northfolk's older beliefs actually are, and whether they're *truer* than the heartland faith (Layer 8/13).
sources: Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: flesh the human regional peoples.
codex-world-builder Layer 3.

entries.name: Peoples of Verranth

sections.body: Who lives on Verranth. Predominantly human, organized by region and outlook, with **a few non-human peoples** (Stephen's direction). Relations are at **moderate friction** — real prejudices and rivalries (above all the devout heartland vs. the skeptical east and the resentful, firesalt-holding south), but peoples trade, mix, and intermarry.

**Human peoples:**
- **[[heartlanders]]** — devout, prosperous, prophecy-cultured westerners; Evan's and Cassian's people. The "we are the protagonists" culture.
- **[[eastland-clans]]** — mobile horse clans of the leeward steppe; skeptical of prophecy; the continent's outside view.
- **[[southerners]]** — coastal traders and Cinderfoot miners who hold the [[firesalt]]; irreverent, resentful of heartland piety.
- **[[northfolk]]** — hard, isolated taiga people who keep older beliefs than the prophecy faith.
- **[[archipelago-folk]]** — independent free-port sea-traders who (with the Meren) hold the [[the-sundering-sea]] strait.

**Non-human peoples:**
- **[[cairn-folk]]** — stony, long-lived highland miners/masons of the [[hagback-mountains]].
- **[[briarkin]]** — small, secretive, magic-adept fae of [[the-tangle]]; tricksters tied to wild drakes and old magic.
- **[[emberkin]]** — drake-blooded, fire-touched people of the volcanic [[the-south]]; attuned to firesalt and drakes.
- **[[meren]]** — amphibious sea-people of the archipelago; aloof traders who help control the strait.

- **Languages (working):** a heartland-based lingua franca carries trade and the prophecy liturgy across the continent; the eastlands, south, north, and non-human peoples keep their own tongues. (Promote to its own Lore entry if language becomes plot-relevant.)
- Moderate friction means alliances of convenience and prejudice, not open race-war — good for comedy and politics both.

- Roster confirmed (Stephen, 2026-06-17): four non-human peoples (Cairn-folk, Briarkin, Emberkin, Meren). Their *nature/mechanics* (how magical, lifespans) still to set with Layer 9.
- Whether any people is also an organized political body needing a Factions entry (Layer 4).

- Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17: humans + a few non-human peoples; moderate friction; Evan and Cassian are Heartlanders.
- codex-world-builder Layer 3.

threads: Roster confirmed (Stephen, 2026-06-17): four non-human peoples (Cairn-folk, Briarkin, Emberkin, Meren). Their *nature/mechanics* (how magical, lifespans) still to set with Layer 9.
Whether any people is also an organized political body needing a Factions entry (Layer 4).
