**In-world usage:** who actually says "the Quick"? (Working: scholars/mages like [[aldous-pemberquick|Pemberquick]] and the [[briarkin]]; the faith tends to fold the idea into "the Pattern.") The gap between the heartland's grand sacred word and this plain true one is thematic.
Whether the wild drakes (and the [[the-tangle|Tangle]]'s creatures) work the Quick the same native way — almost certainly; confirm.
Whether a numbed Quick can be *locally* revived (e.g., what Evan does at the Cradle) versus only healed slowly at scale.
**Live tension (Book 1):** the heresy is stirring now around Evan's case; the Concordance is suppressing any public link between him and it. See [[state-of-the-world]].

**⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY (locked, 2026-06-17): the heresy is correct.** There is no fixed Pattern; free will is real (full truth + the still-open mechanism in [[prophecy-and-foresight]]). Hidden from cast and reader until reveal — this entry's body stays framed as "heresy" on the page.
Whether there's an organized heretical movement, or only scattered believers (could become a Faction later).
Whether anyone deliberately engineered Evan's case to *prove* the heresy.

**⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY — REVISED 2026-06-18 (supersedes the earlier "self-fulfilling" note; Stephen's direction, flagged as a deliberate change to locked canon).** Under the resolved Cradle mechanism (oracle of systems — see [[prophecy-and-foresight]]), the Vindication was **a real systemic warning that Halden genuinely acted on.** The Cradle forecast a real coming threat (working: the Riven War / its logistics); Halden, acting on that practical early-warning, truly helped avert or win it. So the *event* was real, not staged — the founding **lie is the interpretation**: the Concordance claims it proves divine, foreordained fate and the Pattern, when it only ever proved that the Cradle forecasts systems and that people can *act on* a forecast. (**Confirmed by Stephen, 2026-06-18:** this fully supersedes the prior "self-fulfilling / belief produced the outcome" framing — no self-fulfilling element remains. The event was real; only the divine-fate interpretation is the lie.)

**⚠ AUTHOR-ONLY (added 2026-06-18):** Halden became Bearer the same blind way Evan did — he touched the naming-stone (see [[augurite]] naming rule). The faith remembers a destined hero stepping forward to the Pattern's call; the truth is a sanctioned candidate putting his hand on a rock. The difference between Halden and Evan is not fate — it's that Halden was the one *meant* to touch it, and the world he was supposed to save happened to hold together. The parallel is a key crack Book 1 can edge toward.
What really happened to Halden, and whether any record of the truth survives.
Notable free-companies and their captains (Layer 12).
The last war the heartland fought, and against whom (Layer 10).

meta.title: Style guide
meta.body:
# Style Guide (Codex entries)

*The author's. The continuity-check skill applies these rules to **Codex entries only** — never to the manuscript prose. Stephen edits this file; the skill does not.*

This is the rule-sheet for how entries are written and auto-fixed. For the *novel's* prose voice, see `VoiceStyleGuide.md` and the anti-AI-writing guide at `writing-rules.md` (both in this `Meta/` folder).

## Auto-fixable conventions
- **House spelling:** US English throughout.
- **Headings:** sentence case.
- **Label bullets use colons, not em-dashes:** `**Trait:** description`, never `**Trait** — description`.
- **Cross-links:** `[[entry-slug]]`; links are reciprocal (if A links B, B links A).
- **Status vocabulary:** `seed` / `sketch` / `canon` only.
- **Citations:** every load-bearing fact cites its source (chapter/line, codex file, decisions log, or Stephen's direction).

## Naming conventions (locked)
- **Heartlander names — class-coded split (intentional; locked 2026-06-17).** Great houses and grand notables carry whimsical, ornate surnames (e.g., Pemberquick, Pellamy, Tarr); commoners, clerks, and martial-order members carry blunt, terse names (e.g., Orrec, Roun, Vell, or mononyms like Evan, Doss, Wick). The two registers are deliberate texture, not drift — do not "standardize" them away.
- **Cassian Tarr:** male (locked); given name still provisional.
- *(Add canonical spellings of other names, places, factions, and terms here as they settle — e.g. "Canonical spelling X, never Y.")*

## Don't
- Don't invent canon to fill a field — leave it empty and flag.
- Don't resolve a deliberate in-world mystery; log it under Open Threads.
- Don't edit the manuscript.

meta.title: Genre notes
meta.body:
# Genre Notes

*Genre, series shape, themes, and tone — context for continuity judgement. Seed captured 2026-06-17 via the codex-world-builder skill; confirmed by Stephen. World mode: **fictional** (entirely invented).*

## Genre
Comedic fantasy. Humor-forward and absurd-but-warm; stakes are real but the laughs lead. High-magic secondary world.

## World mode & build
- **Mode:** fictional (invented world, not historical/hybrid).
- **Scale:** continental — multiple kingdoms/cultures.
- **Build:** collaborative (layer-by-layer validation) via codex-world-builder.

## Setting register
- **Tech:** "magitech patchwork." Everyday life looks broadly medieval, but high magic does the work industrialization otherwise would (magical light, message-sending, transit, record-keeping). The result is uneven and anachronistic: institutions can feel oddly modern — most importantly, a literal **prophecy bureaucracy** sits at the center of the premise.
- **Magic:** high magic — common and powerful, woven through daily life and institutions. (System designed in Layer 9.)

## Series
[Series name TBD.]
- Book 1: *What to do When You've Broken a Prophecy.*
- Later books: TBD. The "real Chosen One vs. the wrong man" engine is built to run across a series.

## Book 1 in one line
A continent runs on prophecy — and when the great prophecy lands on the wrong man, Evan, the chronicler-Narrator bound to it can only watch, narrate, and despair as the real prophecy goes off the rails.

## Premise (the central pressure)
- Prophecy is load-bearing infrastructure: institutions, faiths, and powers are organized around at least one sweeping, sacred prophecy.
- Each true prophecy comes with a **Narrator** — a chronicler bound to record its fulfillment (see `Characters/narrator.md`).
- **The wrong man:** Evan is mistaken for / swept into the role of the Chosen One. He is *not* the prophesied hero. The real prophecy is now broken or misrouted.
- **The real Chosen One exists and matters** — a live part of the plot/cast (out there, possibly hunting Evan or sidelined). To be built in Layer 12.

## Thematic spine (LOCKED 2026-06-18)
Three braided threads:
- **Fate vs. accident (free will).** The world believes in a Pattern; the truth (author-only) is there is none. Evan is named by blind accident — the living argument that no fate chose him.
- **Does the role make the person, or the person the role?** Evan is handed a role he never earned; the book asks whether it transforms him or merely reveals who he already was.
- **The story you're "supposed" to live vs. the real one.** The sacred script (and the Narrator) insist on how it *should* go; Evan keeps living the actual, off-script story instead.

**The spine's central question (Stephen, 2026-06-18):** *Would Evan have walked the same path if he didn't bump the stone?* Everything above lives in that question.

**Stance (LOCKED 2026-06-18): character answers; philosophy stays open.** Literally, by canon, the answer is *no* — without the accident no prophecy ever finds him (the augurite is blind; no fate seeks him out). But what *mattered* once he was named flowed from who he already was — so the **human** answer is that he *made* the role, it revealed him; he was the right person for the moment even though no fate chose him (the anti-Chosen-One thesis: no right *man* fate picks, but a right *person* found by accident and proven by choice). The deeper **fate-vs-accident** question stays deliberately unresolved: the world (and the Narrator) insist on fate, the buried truth is accident, and the reader is left holding both. This matches the reveal pacing — Book 1 cracks the secret, never confirms it.

## Tone & structure
- **Comps:** Discworld (Pratchett) — warm, satirical, deeply built world where jokes carry real ideas; and The Princess Bride — fairy-tale adventure with a wry framing narrator and heart.
- **POV:** close third on **Evan**, interwoven with the **Narrator's** live interjections (two distinct registers — see `Meta/VoiceStyleGuide.md`).
- **Tense:** present tense — the Narrator chronicles as events happen, which fuels his exasperation when Evan deviates.
- **The comic engine:** Narrator states how the sacred sequence is *supposed* to go → Evan does something else → Narrator, powerless to intervene, can only pronounce how it *should* have gone.

## Non-negotiables / locked decisions
- Evan is the wrong man; the real Chosen One exists.
- The Narrator cannot influence events (states how things should go / should have gone); may address the reader; present tense.
- "Overworked/underpaid" Narrator framing is tonal, not a literal employer.
- Magic = the Craft, fueled by firesalt; true foresight is Cradle-only (see `Lore/the-craft.md`, `Lore/prophecy-and-foresight.md`).
- **The Quick** (locked/named 2026-06-18): all magic acts on a living medium, **the Quick**. The Briarkin work it the native, trained way; **firesalt is a shortcut/steroid** that forces it, and chronic overuse **numbs** the Quick — which is the Long Dark. Entry: `Lore/the-quick.md`.
- **⚠ SPOILER — series core secret (author-only):** the prophecy faith is false; there is no fixed Pattern (the heresy is right). Locked 2026-06-17; full truth in `Lore/prophecy-and-foresight.md`. Hidden from cast/reader until reveal.
- **The great prophecy = the Long Dark** (locked 2026-06-18): a genuinely real foretold doom — the firesalt fires fail in YC 490 — that Evan is named to avert. Full entry: `Lore/the-long-dark.md`.
- **Augurite names whoever touches it** (locked 2026-06-18): a charged naming-stone fixes its Bearer by blind contact — no discernment. Every Bearer in history, Halden included, was simply first to touch the rock (the author-only proof of the no-Pattern secret). Evan is named when he trips and bumps the stone. Bearers are conferred only through a controlled **naming rite** where a single sanctioned candidate is permitted to touch the charged stone (locked 2026-06-18). Full rule: `Objects/augurite.md`.
- **The Presentation failure** (locked 2026-06-18): the public ceremony meant to parade the obedient Bearer (bound south to save the fires) is where Evan instead uses his irrevocable Bearer authority to declare, publicly, for the Tangle — the Concordance cannot override him. He slips the leash; the disaster is that the wrong man *wields* the role rather than flubbing it. Detail: `Notes/outline.md` Part II.
- [Add further hard constraints as Stephen sets them.]

## Length targets
Total 80,000–100,000 words (soft ceiling 100k — confirm with Stephen). Chapters 2,000–2,500 words.

meta.title: Voice & style
meta.body:
# Voice & Style Guide — What to do When You've Broken a Prophecy

*The prose voice guide for the manuscript. (Codex entries follow a separate rule-sheet at `style-guide.md`; the anti-AI-writing guide is at `writing-rules.md`.) This starts as a skeleton — fill each section in as the voice firms up. Stephen's approved prose is the final authority; when a passage departs from this guide, note the divergence and update the guide.*

## POV & tense
Close third on **Evan**, interwoven with **Narrator** interjections (Stephen's direction, 2026-06-17). **Present tense** — the Narrator is chronicling events as they happen, in real time, so the prose runs live rather than recollected. Two registers share the page: the close-third lens on Evan and the Narrator's running chronicle. One protagonist POV (Evan); the Narrator is a second voice, not a second POV character.

## POV registers
**Evan (close third):** *Seeded 2026-06-18 from `Manuscript/ch01.md`.* Present tense, tight on Evan. Plain, deflecting, self-effacing; he never dramatizes himself. He finds genuine relief in small fixable problems ("here at last is a problem the right size") and his competence is clerical, rendered lovingly and precisely (filing by date/seal/name three keys deep). **His stress tell is clerkly, not bodily:** when something frightens him he squares paper corners (*tk, tk*), counts without deciding to, latches onto a trivial procedural task — never jaw/throat/chest. His native idiom is bureaucratic: he literally files a frightening thought "under *not today*, cross-referenced *not me*." Sensory grounding is small and concrete (vellum and lamp-oil, the feeder-rod, the drake in its cage). The narration occasionally notes what Evan fails to notice about himself — gentle dramatic irony from the Narrator's vantage, never Evan congratulating himself.

**The Narrator (chronicle / interjection voice):** Vaguely British, dry, terminally sarcastic; carries himself like an overworked, underpaid functionary (a *tone*, not a literal job); capable of real grandeur for the *correct* prophecy, deflated the instant Evan's actual conduct intrudes. Because he chronicles in present tense, he repeatedly predicts the prophesied beat and is caught out when Evan does something else — the comedy is that live gap, surfacing as genuine exasperation. He knows he's keeping the record (he may acknowledge the reader and that he's chronicling), but he's a chronicler aware of his record, not a comedian working a crowd. Calibrated to The Stanley Parable narrator for cadence, wit, and frustrated-puppeteer energy. See `Characters/narrator.md`.

- Keep the two registers audibly distinct: Evan's lens is grounded and in-scene; the Narrator's sits a half-step above it, predicting and reacting.
- The engine to lean on: Narrator states what's supposed to happen → Evan breaks it / ignores it → Narrator's exasperation. Use in present tense.
- He **cannot influence events** — his only power is pronouncing how things should go, or should have gone. Flag any draft where he changes what Evan does.
- He **may address the reader**. Open only: how often he does so.

## Voice principles
*Seeded 2026-06-18 from the Narrator's draft opening (`Manuscript/00-opening-narration.md`). These describe the Narrator register; Evan's close-third principles get filled once his lens is drafted.*

- **Grandeur must be earned with concrete images, never puffery.** The Narrator is pompous — but his pomp lands through specifics ("stood at the shoulder of kings in their last hour," "the way your world runs on grain and spite"), not generic awe ("a rich tapestry of prophecy"). His grandiosity is a *character trait*, not the prose's default setting. The moment it goes vague, it stops being funny and starts sounding like the very puffery in `writing-rules.md` (A1/A6).
- **The deflation beat is the engine.** Build a long, rolling, self-important sentence, then puncture it with a short flat one. ("…who will stride into the dark at the appointed hour and —" / "No. Not yet." …or the kicker: "Do try to contain yourselves.") The comedy lives in the drop. Pair almost every flight of grandeur with a fall.
- **Dramatic irony is structural.** The Narrator overvalues the grand "correct" version and the hero he's expecting, and undervalues Evan — he is backing the wrong horse and doesn't know it. Let his confidence be the joke; never have him wink at what he's missing.
- **He knows the script, not the future (LOCKED, Stephen 2026-06-18).** The Narrator knows how the story is *supposed* to go — the expected/intended narrative — **not what actually happens.** He must never foreshadow a deviation or reveal an outcome he hasn't reached: no "he was right about exactly one of those things," no "the man the doom will be handed to tomorrow." He states the *expected* beat with full confidence ("the Pattern points to Cassian Tarr… it is going to be perfect") and is blindsided live when Evan breaks it. He may foreshadow what the prophecy/script openly contains (a doom is coming; a hero will be named) — never the accidents to come. The irony is the reader's; the Narrator never winks.
- **Direct address, used with control.** He may speak to "you"/the reader, as a chronicler aware he's keeping the record — not a comedian working a crowd. A little goes far.
- **Present tense, live and catchable.** He narrates as it happens, states what's *supposed* to come, and gets caught out when Evan does otherwise. The exasperation is in real time, not after-the-fact wit.
- **Em-dash = self-interruption, on purpose.** He cuts himself off when reality intrudes ("and —"). It's a deliberate device for that beat, not a default rhythm; don't let dashes pepper every sentence.
- **Rhythm: vary length hard.** Long periodic sentences for the grandeur; short sentences for the deflation and the mundane. The whiplash between the two is the sound of the book.
- **Warm under the sarcasm.** Princess Bride, not contempt. He's exasperated and vain, but the telling is affectionate enough that we enjoy his company. Never cruel to Evan; just convinced he's a waste of a good prologue.

## Dialogue
[How characters speak; any conventions for non-human or non-standard speech; punctuation choices.]

## Things to avoid
- Everything in `writing-rules.md` — and watch the grand register especially: the Narrator's pomposity is a magnet for **A1 puffery** and **A6 inflated vocabulary** ("tapestry," "testament," "realm," "journey," "weave" used straight). His grandeur must come from specific images, or it reads as the exact AI-tell the book should never trip.
- Don't let the deflation curdle into meanness. The Narrator mocks the *situation* and his own bad luck, not Evan's worth.
- Don't overuse direct address or em-dash interruptions; both are seasoning, not the dish.
- When Evan's close-third arrives: no generic physical tells (jaw/throat/chest), no "something shifted" interiority — give him a specific, clerkly stress response (to be defined with his register).

## Worked example — the Narrator's opening (draft, pending approval)
The reference text the principles above are drawn from. Full draft: `Manuscript/00-opening-narration.md`. Note how it works: three grand sentences to introduce himself → "Oh, this one is going to be *magnificent*" → a build toward the hero, cut off mid-sentence ("and —") → the rationalization for slumming it ("the price of the view") → the hard landing on the mundane ("We begin with Evan. He is a clerk… he is filing.") → the deflating button ("Do try to contain yourselves."). Grandeur, then drop; the hero he's hyping is the wrong man, and he has no idea.

> I have narrated the fall of cities. I have stood at the shoulder of kings in their last hour and told that hour, exactly, as it fell. […] And a hero — *the* hero, trained from his first breath for the breaking of precisely this night, who will stride into the dark at the appointed hour and —
> No. Not yet. […]
> So we do not begin with the hero. We begin with Evan. He is a clerk.

## Open questions
- ~~Evan's close-third register~~ — **seeded 2026-06-18** (see POV registers above; from `ch01.md`). Refine as more of him is drafted.
- ~~How the page hands off between Narrator and close-third~~ — **decided 2026-06-18.** It is **one chronicle**: the default mode is close-third effaced onto Evan (third person), and the **Narrator breaks through in first person ("I"), set off as its own paragraph(s)**, sitting a half-step above the scene and free to say what Evan can't see ("that lamp is the first sentence of the doom"). A horizontal rule marks the move from the pure-Narrator overture into the close-third at a chapter's head; mid-scene, the person/voice shift alone marks his break-ins (no rule needed). He foretells and editorializes but never changes events.
- Frequency of direct address once underway — Ch. 1 uses it sparingly (two Narrator break-ins mid-scene + the overture). Watch the density across chapters; keep it a spice.
